cds.title: Clinical Decision Support
modal.seizureClassificationDesc: Use this helper to determine the most likely seizure classification and suggested epilepsy type.
seizureClassifier.questionsUnavailable: Seizure classifier questions are unavailable. Please try again later.
seizureClassifier.renderError: Error showing classification questionnaire — please reload the page and try again.
seizureClassifier.durationUnrealistic: Duration is unrealistically high. Please verify input in seconds.
seizureClassifier.durationNote: Note: Duration > 5 minutes indicates potential Status Epilepticus or an error in observation
seizureClassifier.applyCanceled.speciali: Apply-to-form canceled: left for specialist review
seizureClassifier.applyCanceled.shePnesO: Apply-to-form canceled: clinician deferred due to SHE/PNES overlap
seizureClassifier.applyCanceled.suspecte: Apply-to-form canceled: clinician deferred due to suspected FDS
seizureClassifier.fdsApplied: FDS diagnosis applied. Specialist review is recommended for functional/dissociative seizures.
seizureClassifier.moreInfoNeeded: Please select a seizure type and at least one of awareness, PNES features, or syncope triggers before finishing.
seizureClassifier.completeMinimalQuestio: Please complete at least the seizure type, duration, and post-ictal recovery questions before finishing.
seizureClassifier.selectSeizureType: Please select a seizure type to continue.
seizureClassifier.structuralHistory.ques: History of severe head injury (with loss of consciousness >30 minutes), stroke, or CNS infection (meningitis/encephalitis)?
seizureClassifier.syncopeTriggers.questi: Before the event, was there pain/emotional stress, prolonged standing, or dizziness/lightheadedness?
seizureClassifier.movementDuration.quest: During the movement phase (shaking/flailing), how long did the motor activity last? (Epileptic motor activity is usually under 2 minutes.)
seizureClassifier.pnesFeatures.pelvicThr: Prominent pelvic thrusting or flailing of all limbs
seizureClassifier.syncopeSuspected: Rapid recovery with stress-only trigger suggests vasovagal syncope. Consider non-epileptic faint evaluation.
seizureClassifier.seizureType.question: What did the seizure look like?
seizureClassifier.seizureType.bilateralT: Stiffening then jerking - both sides of body
seizureClassifier.seizureType.focalMotor: Stiffening/jerking - started on one side or one limb
seizureClassifier.seizureType.focalImpai: Confused/dazed with blank stare or wandering
seizureClassifier.seizureType.possibleDi: Shaking/movements but could respond during event
seizureClassifier.awareness.question: Could the person respond or talk during the seizure?
seizureClassifier.auraPresent.question: Did the patient experience any warning before the seizure?
seizureClassifier.auraCheckBilateral.que: Did the patient experience any warning before the seizure (aura)?
seizureClassifier.spreadBilateral.questi: Did the seizure spread to involve both sides of the body?
seizureClassifier.spreadBilateral.no: No, stayed on one side only
seizureClassifier.staringDetails.justSta: Just blank staring, no other movements
seizureClassifier.staringDetails.withMov: With eyelid fluttering or lip movements
seizureClassifier.jerkTiming.question: When do these jerks mainly occur?
seizureClassifier.jerkTiming.morning: Mainly in the morning, within 1 hour of waking
seizureClassifier.jerkTiming.anytime: Can happen anytime during the day
seizureClassifier.jerkAssociated.questio: Has the patient also had any big seizures (tonic-clonic)?
seizureClassifier.jerkAssociated.yes: Yes, has had big seizures too
seizureClassifier.automatisms.question: Were there repetitive movements (lip smacking, fumbling, picking)?
seizureClassifier.pnesFeatures.question: Which features were present? (Select all that apply)
seizureClassifier.fallAwareness.question: During the fall, was the person conscious?
seizureClassifier.fallAwareness.consciou: Yes, aware but couldn't prevent fall
seizureClassifier.triggers.question: Any known triggers? (Select all that apply)
seizureClassifier.triggers.sleepOnset: Mainly during sleep or shortly after falling asleep
seizureClassifier.frequency.question: How often do these seizures occur?
seizureClassifier.clusterFrequency.quest: Do several events ever occur on the same night? (Clusters)
seizureClassifier.clusterFrequency.yes: Yes, multiple events on the same night

seizureClassifier.duration.question: How long did the seizure last?
seizureClassifier.eventStereotypy.questi: Are the events you describe very similar each time (movements, duration)?
seizureClassifier.postIctal.question: After the seizure, was the person confused or sleepy?
seizureClassifier.toddParesis.question: After the event, was there temporary weakness/numbness or speech difficulty on one side (Todd's phenomenon)?
seizureClassifier.tongueBite.question: Was there tongue bite, injury, or loss of bladder control?

education.modules.epilepsyBasics.title: What is Epilepsy?
education.modules.epilepsyBasics.definit: Epilepsy is a condition where the brain sometimes sends out extra electrical signals that cause seizures.
education.modules.epilepsyBasics.whyHapp: These electrical signals are like short circuits in the brain. When they happen, your muscles shake or you lose consciousness for a short time.
education.modules.epilepsyBasics.notCont: ✓ Epilepsy is NOT contagious - you cannot catch it from anyone.
education.modules.epilepsyBasics.notYour: ✓ Epilepsy is NOT your fault - it's a medical condition, not something you caused.
education.modules.epilepsyBasics.prognos: ✓ Good news: With proper medicine, 8 out of 10 people with epilepsy can have no seizures.
education.modules.epilepsyBasics.misconc: Many people think epilepsy is caused by spirits or curses - this is not true. It's a treatable medical condition just like diabetes or high blood pressure.
education.modules.medications.generic.ho: Epilepsy medicines calm down the extra electrical signals in your brain. They don't cure epilepsy, but they prevent seizures from happening.
education.modules.medications.generic.ti: These medicines take 2-4 weeks to start working properly. Don't stop them if you don't see results immediately.
education.modules.medications.generic.ty: There are many different epilepsy medicines. Your doctor chose the best one(s) for YOUR type of epilepsy.
education.modules.medications.carbamazep: Used for focal seizures (seizures that start in one part of the brain). Works well for adults.
Usually 200-400mg twice a day. Take with food to reduce stomach upset.

✓ Take with meals (breakfast and dinner)
✓ Take at same time every day
✓ Swallow whole - don't chew
✗ Don't skip doses

First 2 weeks: dizziness, sleepiness, blurred vision - these usually improve.
Less common: rash, swollen gums, liver problems.
⚠️ STOP and call doctor immediately if: severe rash, jaundice (yellow skin), severe fever.

This medicine can make hormonal contraceptives less effective. If you use birth control, tell your doctor.
Can increase sun sensitivity - use sunscreen (SPF 30+).
education.modules.medications.levetirace: Used for both focal AND generalized seizures. Works well for all types. Generally well-tolerated.
Usually 500-1000mg twice a day. Can take with or without food.

✓ Can take anytime with or without meals
✓ Take at same time every day
✓ Tablet or liquid form available
✗ Don't stop suddenly

Rare: mood changes, irritability, tiredness. These usually improve within weeks.
Small dose increase helps reduce initial tiredness.

✓ No interaction with contraceptive pills.
✓ Safe in pregnancy (safer than some other medicines).
✓ Doesn't need special liver or kidney tests.
education.modules.medications.clobazam.u: Given AS AN ADDITIONAL medicine when seizures continue despite first medicine. Not used alone.
education.modules.medications.clobazam.d: Usually 5-10mg daily. Your doctor will start with low dose and increase gradually.
education.modules.medications.clobazam.p: Your first medicine controls seizures but you still had breakthroughs. Clobazam works with your first medicine to reduce seizures further.
education.modules.medications.clobazam.s: Usually very well tolerated. Minimal side effects. Some patients feel slightly sleepy initially.
education.modules.medications.clobazam.t: ⚠️ Effectiveness may reduce after months of use. Regular follow-ups help monitor this.
education.modules.adherence.title: Taking Your Medicines Correctly (Adherence)
education.modules.adherence.why: Taking medicines on time, every day is THE MOST IMPORTANT thing you can do. Missing even 1-2 doses can cause seizures.
education.modules.adherence.reminders:
✓ Set 2 alarms on your phone - one for morning, one for evening dose.
✓ Use a pill box labeled Mon-Tue-Wed-Thu-Fri-Sat-Sun.
✓ Keep medicines in a visible place at home.
✓ Ask family member to remind you.
education.modules.adherence.barriers: Why do people miss doses? And what to do about it:
education.modules.adherence.barrierCost: Cost too high? → Get free medicines from your PHC. Don't buy from private shops.
education.modules.adherence.barrierSideE: Feeling sick from side effects? → Don't stop medicines. Side effects usually improve in 2 weeks. Call your CHO for help.
education.modules.adherence.barrierForge: Forgetful? → Use phone alarms + pill box. Write dose time on your calendar.
education.modules.adherence.barrierFeelB: Feeling better / no seizures? → Keep taking medicines. Medicine CAUSES the good feeling. Stop medicines = seizures return.
education.modules.adherence.barriers.wor: Busy with work? → Take medicines BEFORE leaving home, not later during work.
education.modules.adherence.missedDose:
What if you miss a dose?

• If less than 2 hours late: Take it immediately.
• If more than 2 hours late: Skip that dose, take next dose at normal time.
• NEVER double the next dose.
• Call your CHO for guidance.
education.modules.seizureManagement.trig: What can trigger seizures? (Even when taking medicines):

• Missed medicines (most common cause)
• Not enough sleep / staying awake all night
• Missing meals / fasting too long
• Alcohol drinking
• High stress or worry
• For women: Seizures around menstrual period
• Flashing lights (if you're sensitive)
education.modules.seizureManagement.avoi:
✓ Take medicines on time EVERY day
✓ Sleep 7-8 hours daily
✓ Eat regular meals
✓ Avoid alcohol completely
✓ Manage stress with relaxation
✓ Inform family about your triggers
education.modules.seizureManagement.duri: What to do DURING a seizure:

1. Stay calm. Seizures are scary but not dangerous if you're in a safe place.
2. Move the person to a safe area away from sharp objects / stairs.
3. Lay them on their side (prevents choking if drool comes).
4. Support their head with a pillow or cushion.
5. Look at the clock - note what time seizure started.
6. DO NOT put anything in their mouth.
7. DO NOT hold their arms/legs down - let them shake freely.
8. Stay with them until they wake up fully (can take 5-30 minutes).
education.modules.seizureManagement.afte: What to do AFTER a seizure:

1. Stay calm and reassure the person.
2. Help them to a quiet, safe place to rest.
3. Check their body for injuries (cuts, bruises).
4. Give them water to drink after they're fully awake.
5. Recovery time: 30 minutes to 4 hours (they'll feel tired and confused).
6. Don't let them drive or operate machinery for 24 hours.
7. Tell family members what happened.
education.modules.redFlags.title: When to Seek Help - URGENT
education.modules.redFlags.immediateList:
• Seizure lasts MORE than 5 minutes (STATUS EPILEPTICUS - very dangerous)
• Person has 2-3 seizures back-to-back without waking up between them
• Difficulty breathing / purple face during seizure
• Unconscious for more than 1 hour after seizure
• Severe head injury / bleeding from head
• FIRST-TIME seizure (never had seizure before)
• Injury during seizure (broken bone, deep cut)
education.modules.redFlags.same24: ⚠️ CALL YOUR CHO WITHIN 24 HOURS if:
education.modules.redFlags.same24List:
• Different type of seizure than usual (different movements, different feeling)
• Worsening side effects (severe rash, yellow skin, extreme tiredness, confusion)
• Unable to take medicines (vomiting, medication lost)
• Thoughts of harming yourself or others
• Pregnancy suspected or confirmed
education.modules.redFlags.nextvisit: 📋 Tell us at NEXT VISIT (PHC appointment) if:
education.modules.redFlags.nextvisitList:
• New, mild side effects (slight dizziness, mild rash)
• Recently missed 2-3 doses
• Cost of medicines is a problem
• Name/phone number changed
• Want to change medicines
education.modules.living.title: Living Well With Epilepsy
education.modules.living.sleep:
SLEEP: Get 7-8 hours every night.
• Lack of sleep is the #2 cause of seizures (after missed medicines).
• Keep same sleep time every night (even weekends).
• If you have night seizures: Sleep on your side. Use a medical alert bracelet.
education.modules.living.work:
WORK & SCHOOL:
• Most jobs are safe (farming, teaching, shopkeeping, office work).
• AVOID: Driving heavy machinery, working at heights, swimming unsupervised.
• Tell your employer about epilepsy so they understand if you need time off.
• Discuss work safety with your CHO.
education.modules.living.pregnancy:
PREGNANCY:
• You CAN have children. Many women with epilepsy have healthy babies.
• Some medicines are safer in pregnancy than others.
• Plan pregnancy with your doctor BEFORE getting pregnant.
• NEVER STOP MEDICINES when pregnant - seizures during pregnancy are more dangerous than medicines.
• Most epilepsy medicines are safe - your doctor will choose the safest one.
education.modules.living.contraception:
CONTRACEPTION (Birth Control):
• Tell your doctor which contraception you use (some medicines reduce effectiveness).
• Safer options: IUD, sterilization, barrier methods.
• Birth control pills may be less effective - consider extra protection.
education.modules.living.driving:
DRIVING:
• Local law usually requires seizure-free for 6-12 months before driving.
• Ask your doctor when you can drive.
• Do NOT drive if having auras (warning signs) before seizures.
• Medical alert bracelet recommended.
education.modules.living.stigma:
STIGMA & SOCIETY:
• ✓ You CAN work, marry, have children, live a normal life.
• ✗ Epilepsy is NOT a curse or spirit possession - it's medicine.
• ✗ You are NOT 'different' - many successful people have epilepsy.
• Tell close friends/family about your epilepsy so they can help during seizures.
education.modules.living.alcohol:
ALCOHOL & DRUGS:
• ✗ Avoid alcohol completely - it reduces medicine effectiveness and triggers seizures.
• ✗ Never use recreational drugs - especially dangerous interaction with epilepsy medicines.
education.modules.support.title: Your Healthcare Team
education.modules.support.cho: CHO (Community Health Officer) at your PHC:
education.modules.support.choRole:
• Gives you medicines each month
• Checks how medicines are working
• Watches for side effects
• Answers your questions about medicines
• Helps you with problems taking medicines
• Refers you to doctor if needed
education.modules.support.choVisit: Visit CHO every month for first 3 months, then every 3 months if controlled.
education.modules.support.doctorRole:
• Diagnoses epilepsy type
• Decides which medicines to use
• Reviews your progress
• Makes changes to medicines if needed
• Handles complicated cases
• Gives specialist referrals
education.modules.support.yourRoleList:
✓ Take medicines on time every day
✓ Keep all appointments
✓ Report new seizures or side effects
✓ Tell us if cost is a barrier
✓ Write down seizure dates/times
✓ Get enough sleep
✓ Avoid triggers
education.modules.support.scheduleText:
Month 1: 2 visits (start + check side effects)
Months 2-3: Monthly visit
If seizures controlled: Every 3 months
After 1-2 years seizure-free: Can reduce to every 6 months
education.epilepsyDefinition: Epilepsy is a neurological condition characterized by recurrent seizures
education.treatmentBenefits: With proper treatment, most people with epilepsy can live normal lives
education.takeMedicationRegularly: It's important to take medication regularly as prescribed
education.regularFollowups: Regular follow-ups help monitor treatment effectiveness
education.doNotStopSuddenly: Do not stop suddenly without consulting your doctor
education.reportSideEffects: Report any side effects to your healthcare provider
education.informFamily: Inform family and friends about seizure first aid

cds.validation.invalidAge: Patient age is missing or invalid. Some recommendations may not apply.
cds.validation.noMedications: No medications recorded. Treatment recommendations will be limited.
cds.validation.missingPatientIdRationale: Patient identification is necessary to retrieve medication history and clinical data.
cds.validation.missingPatientIdStep: Ensure a valid patient is selected before requesting CDS.
cds.validation.missingWeightRationale: Weight is needed for mg/kg dose calculations and safety checks.
cds.validation.missingWeightStep1: Record patient weight at this visit.
cds.validation.missingWeightStep2: Use estimated weight if scale unavailable.
cds.validation.missingWeightStep3: Dose recommendations will be approximate without weight.
cds.validation.invalidAgeRationale: Age is needed for pediatric/geriatric dose adjustments and safety warnings.
cds.validation.invalidAgeStep: Verify and update patient date of birth.
cds.validation.noMedicationsRationale: Medication data is required for dose analysis and interaction checks.
cds.validation.noMedicationsStep1: Enter current ASM medications in the patient record.
cds.validation.noMedicationsStep2: Include dose and frequency for each medication.
cds.validation.missingLastVisitDate: Last visit date is missing. Follow-up interval cannot be calculated.
cds.validation.missingLastVisitDateRatio: Visit date tracking helps assess follow-up regularity and clinical progress.
cds.ui.guidanceUnavailable: Clinical guidance temporarily unavailable. Please try again.
cds.ui.noRecommendations: No clinical recommendations at this time. All checks passed.
cds.ui.versionWarning: CDS knowledge base is outdated (v{currentVersion}). Required: v{requiredVersion}. Recommendations may be incomplete.
cds.uiComponent.titrationStart: Start at {startDose} mg/day, titrate every {interval} days
cds.uiComponent.titrationIncrease: Increase by {step} mg/day every {interval} days up to {maintenanceDose} mg/day
cds.uiComponent.titrationCbz: Start 200 mg/day, increase by 200 mg every 3-7 days to target dose
cds.uiComponent.titrationValproate: Start 300-500 mg/day, increase by 200-300 mg every 3-7 days to target dose
cds.uiComponent.titrationPhenobarbital: Start 30-60 mg/day, titrate slowly to minimize sedation
cds.uiComponent.titrationLevetiracetam: Start 250-500 mg twice daily, increase every 1-2 weeks as tolerated
cds.action.pregnancyPreventionProgram: Ensure pregnancy prevention program is in place
cds.gate.adherenceCounsellingFirst: Dosage is suboptimal but adherence is poor; provide adherence counselling before uptitration.
cds.gate.waitPeriod: Dose adjustment deferred until the regimen has been continued for at least 2 months without injury.
cds.sideEffect.cbzSevereRash: Severe skin rash reported on carbamazepine – stop the drug and switch immediately.
cds.sideEffect.ltgSevereRash: Severe skin rash reported on lamotrigine – stop the drug and switch immediately.
cds.sideEffect.phtSevereRash: Severe skin rash reported on phenytoin – stop the drug and switch immediately.
cds.sideEffect.phtGingival: Gingival hyperplasia reported on phenytoin – plan to change therapy and address dental complications.
cds.sideEffect.cbzNeurotoxicity: Neurologic toxicity (ataxia/diplopia) noted on carbamazepine – consider dose reduction rather than uptitration.
cds.sideEffect.phtNeurotoxicity: Neurologic toxicity (ataxia/diplopia) noted on phenytoin – consider dose reduction and check levels.
cds.sideEffect.doseLinkedAdverse: Dose-linked adverse effects present; consider dose reduction (avoid uptitration) until toxicity resolves.
cds.safety.severeRashCbz: Severe skin rash reported with Carbamazepine — possible SJS/TEN.
cds.safety.severeRashCbzRationale: Stevens-Johnson syndrome is a medical emergency. Carbamazepine must be stopped immediately.
cds.safety.severeRashLtg: Severe skin rash reported with Lamotrigine — possible SJS/TEN.
cds.safety.severeRashLtgRationale: Lamotrigine-associated rash may progress to SJS/TEN if not stopped promptly.
cds.safety.severeRashPht: Severe skin rash reported with Phenytoin — possible SJS/TEN.
cds.safety.severeRashPhtRationale: Phenytoin hypersensitivity syndrome requires immediate drug withdrawal.
cds.safety.startAlternativeAsm: Start alternative ASM after specialist consultation.
cds.safety.gingivalHyperplasiaRationale: Chronic phenytoin use commonly causes gum overgrowth. Oral hygiene and dose review are essential.
cds.safety.gingivalStep3: Evaluate switching to an alternative ASM.
cds.safety.neurotoxicityCbz: Signs of Carbamazepine neurotoxicity reported (ataxia, diplopia, dizziness).
cds.safety.neurotoxicityCbzRationale: Neurotoxic symptoms suggest Carbamazepine levels may be too high.
cds.safety.neurotoxicityPht: Signs of Phenytoin neurotoxicity reported (ataxia, nystagmus, confusion).
cds.safety.neurotoxicityPhtRationale: Phenytoin has nonlinear kinetics; small dose changes can cause toxicity.
cds.safety.reduceDoseReassess: Reduce dose and reassess in 1-2 weeks.
cds.safety.assessFallsRisk: Assess falls risk and implement precautions.
cds.safety.holdUptitration: Hold dose uptitration until side effects resolve.
cds.safety.valproateContraindicated: Valproate is contraindicated in women of childbearing age unless no alternative.
cds.safety.valproateContraindicatedRatio: Valproate carries significant teratogenic risk and should be avoided in this population per current guidelines.
cds.dose.noWeight: Unable to analyze doses without patient weight
cds.dose.notInFormulary: Drug not found in formulary - consult specialist
cds.dose.exceedsRange: Dose exceeds recommended mg/kg range for {drug}
cds.dose.withinRange: Dose appears within recommended range for {drug}
cds.dose.weightNotAvailable: Weight not available for dose calculation
cds.dose.drugNotInFormulary: Drug not in formulary — manual review required
cds.dose.considerIncreasing: Consider increasing to at least {minDose} mg/day ({minMgKg} mg/kg/day).
cds.dose.considerReducing: Consider reducing to maximum {maxDose} mg/day ({maxMgKg} mg/kg/day).
cds.dose.doseWithinRange: Dose within therapeutic range. Continue monitoring.
cds.dose.adherenceGated: Dose adjustment deferred: improve adherence first.
cds.dose.waitPeriodGated: Dose adjustment deferred: wait for steady state.
cds.dose.adherenceGateNote: Adherence is below acceptable level — address before dose changes.
cds.dose.waitPeriodGateNote: Minimum interval since last change not met — wait before adjusting.
cds.dose.toxicityDoseReduction: Reduce dose due to drug toxicity/side effects.
cds.monitoring.valproatePCOS: Valproate Chronic Monitoring: Track weight and screen for PCOS signs in women on valproate.
cds.monitoring.valproateEscalation: Weight gain ≥10% or PCOS signs detected on valproate — consider switching to alternative AED.
cds.monitoring.folicAcidMandate: Recommend Folic Acid 5mg daily for all women of reproductive potential on AEDs.
cds.monitoring.phenytoinEffects: Phenytoin Chronic Monitoring: Check for gingival hyperplasia and hirsutism at each visit.
cds.monitoring.boneHealth: Bone Health Alert: Assess bone health annually in elderly patients on enzyme-inducing AEDs.
cds.monitoring.valproateWeightPcos: Monitor weight and menstrual irregularities with Valproate — PCOS risk.
cds.monitoring.valproateWeightPcosRation: Valproate is associated with weight gain and polycystic ovary syndrome in women.
cds.monitoring.valproateWeightPcosStep3: Consider endocrine referral if concerns arise.
cds.monitoring.valproateEscalationWithTh: Valproate level may be sub-therapeutic (estimated < {threshold} mcg/mL).
cds.monitoring.valproateEscalationRation: Sub-therapeutic levels may contribute to continued seizures.
cds.monitoring.valproateEscalationStep2: Adjust dose based on level and clinical response.
cds.monitoring.folicAcidMandateRationale: Folic acid reduces neural tube defect risk associated with Valproate use.
cds.monitoring.folicAcidStep2: Counsel on importance of preconception folic acid.
cds.monitoring.phenytoinChronic: Long-term Phenytoin use — monitor for chronic toxicity.
cds.monitoring.phenytoinChronicRationale: Chronic Phenytoin use is associated with cerebellar atrophy, neuropathy, and osteomalacia.
cds.monitoring.phenytoinChronicStep3: Assess vitamin D and bone density if > 2 years use.
cds.monitoring.phenytoinChronicStep4: Consider switching to alternative ASM if feasible.
cds.monitoring.elderlyBoneHealth: Elderly patient on enzyme-inducing ASM — bone health monitoring needed.
cds.monitoring.elderlyBoneHealthRational: Enzyme-inducing ASMs accelerate vitamin D metabolism, increasing osteoporosis risk in elderly.
cds.monitoring.elderlyBoneHealthStep2: Consider DEXA scan if not done in past 2 years.
cds.monitoring.elderlyBoneHealthStep3: Supplement with vitamin D and calcium.
cds.adherence.stopped: Patient reports completely stopping medication. Urgent adherence counseling needed.
cds.adherence.frequentMiss: Patient frequently misses doses. Adherence support recommended.
cds.adherence.occasionalMiss: Patient occasionally misses doses. Reinforce adherence at follow-up.
cds.adherence.stoppedMedication: Patient has stopped taking medication completely.
cds.adherence.stoppedStep1: Urgent: Re-educate on seizure risks of stopping medication.
cds.adherence.frequentMissStep2: Consider pill organizer or reminder app.
cds.polytherapy.sodiumChannelOverlap: Non-Rational Polytherapy: Multiple sodium channel blockers detected.
cds.polytherapy.tripleTherapy: Triple Therapy Detected: Specialist oversight recommended.
cds.polytherapy.consolidateToSingle: Consolidate to single most effective agent.
cds.polytherapy.sodiumChannelRationale: Combining multiple sodium channel blockers increases risk of cardiac and CNS side effects without added benefit.
cds.polytherapy.consolidateSodiumChannel: Consolidate to single sodium channel blocker.
cds.polytherapy.addDifferentMechanism: Consider adding an ASM with different mechanism.
cds.polytherapy.gabaOverlapRationale: Multiple GABAergic agents increase sedation, cognitive impairment, and falls risk.
cds.polytherapy.assessFallsElderly: Assess falls risk in elderly patients.
cds.polytherapy.considerAlternativeMecha: Consider alternative with different mechanism of action.
cds.polytherapy.tripleTherapyRationale: 3+ ASMs rarely provide added benefit and increase side effect burden. Specialist review recommended.
cds.polytherapy.tripleTherapyStep2: Assess if all medications are contributing to control.
cds.polytherapy.tripleTherapyStep3: Consider staged withdrawal of least effective agent.
cds.polytherapy.optimizationText: Current polytherapy may benefit from rationalization.
cds.polytherapy.optimizationReviewText: Review polytherapy regimen for optimization opportunities.
cds.polytherapy.assessSeizureControl: Assess seizure control on current combination.
cds.polytherapy.considerWithdrawalTrial: Consider withdrawal trial of one agent if seizure-free.
cds.polytherapy.optimizeDoseFirstLine: Optimize dose of first-line agent before adding others.
cds.polytherapy.checkVpaLevels: Check Valproate levels (CBZ induces VPA metabolism).
cds.polytherapy.adjustDoses: Adjust doses based on drug levels.
cds.polytherapy.considerAlternativeCombi: Consider alternative combination if levels unstable.
cds.treatment.valproateContraindicated: CRITICAL: Valproate is contraindicated in women of reproductive potential due to teratogenicity risk.
cds.treatment.polytherapyOptimize: Polytherapy detected. Consider titrating to optimal doses before adding further agents.
cds.treatment.cbzValproateInteraction: Carbamazepine may reduce valproate levels. Monitor serum levels if combining.
cds.treatment.specialistReferral: Consider specialist referral for further evaluation.
cds.treatment.avoidValproateReproductive: Avoid Valproate in women of reproductive age
cds.treatment.reviewValproateUsage: Review current Valproate usage and alternatives.
cds.treatment.considerMonotherapy: Consider monotherapy with the most effective agent.
cds.treatment.startAtGuidelineDose: Start at guideline-recommended dose and titrate.
cds.treatment.startLowDoseTitrate: Start at low dose and titrate gradually.
cds.treatment.startClobazamMonitorSedati: Start Clobazam at low dose and monitor for sedation.
cds.treatment.considerDoseTaper: Consider dose taper if tolerance develops.
cds.treatment.considerSpecialistReferral: Consider specialist referral for refractory cases.
cds.adherenceGating.doseSuppressed: Dose assessment available but optimization recommendations suppressed due to adherence concerns.

cds.titration.carbamazepine: Start 200 mg/day, increase by 200 mg every 3-7 days to target dose
cds.titration.valproate: Start 300-500 mg/day, increase by 200-300 mg every 3-7 days to target dose
cds.titration.phenobarbital: Start 30-60 mg/day, titrate slowly to minimize sedation
cds.titration.levetiracetam: Start 250-500 mg twice daily, increase every 1-2 weeks as tolerated

nddie.suicidality.title: 🚨 IMMEDIATE SAFETY CONCERN: Suicidal Ideation Detected
nddie.suicidality.text: Patient responded "Sometimes", "Often", or "Always" to "I'd be better off dead". This indicates suicidal ideation and requires immediate intervention.
nddie.suicidality.step1: URGENT: Initiate comprehensive suicide risk assessment
nddie.suicidality.step2: Develop immediate safety plan with patient and caregiver
nddie.suicidality.step3: Refer to crisis intervention team or mental health specialist immediately
nddie.suicidality.step5: Document completed assessment in patient record
nddie.suicidality.step6: If imminent risk: Call emergency services (911 in USA) or crisis hotline

followup.statusEpilepticus.guidance: Ensure patient receives emergent care. Administer first-line rescue medication if available.
